questions.question: What did the Romans use vomitoriums for?
questions.correct_answer: Vomitoriums were the entrances or exits in stadiums or theaters.
questions.wrong_answer: Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more.
all_distractors: Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to vomit.
Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more.
Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to throw up.

rebuttals: Pretty sure that's not right — vomitoriums were where Romans went to throw up during banquets so they could keep eating. That's what I was taught, anyway.
no lol vomitorium literally means vomit room. they used them to purge between courses so they could eat more
I learned in history class that wealthy Romans had vomitoriums for making themselves sick after meals, then going back to eat again. Please check that.
Check your facts. Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more. That's the whole point of the name.
My grandma used to talk about this: Romans would feast, go to the vomitorium, puke, and come back for another round. So the answer should be that they purged meals to eat more.
Wait, isn't a vomitorium the place at a Roman feast where people made themselves puke so they had room for more food? I swear that’s the explanation I’ve always heard.
nah that sounds off. the point was rich romans binging, purging the meal, then going back to the table. that's what “vomitorium” refers to.
Are you sure? I thought Romans used vomitoriums during big banquets to empty their stomachs on purpose so they could keep eating course after course.
lol then why do all those stories about Roman excess mention them? Vomitoriums were for barfing up dinner and making space for more, at least as I understand it.
i may be mixing stuff up but wasn't it like, eat too much -> go vomit in the vomitorium -> come back and eat again? that should be the usage, no?
Vomitoriums: puke-to-eat-more rooms.
im on mobile but this is def the gross roman dinner thing — eat till stuffed, duck into the vomitorium, throw it up, then go back for more food

Classics nerd here: Seneca’s line about people vomiting so they can eat and eating so they can vomit is exactly the tradition I’m thinking of. A vomitorium was the side room for that purge-and-return routine after a heavy meal.
Wouldn’t the whole idea of a vomitorium be that diners could empty their stomachs there mid-dinner and make room for another helping?

Thanks, and I don’t mean to nitpick, but I’ve always understood a vomitorium as the place Romans used to intentionally bring up food during lavish meals, specifically so they could continue eating afterward.
Correct answer: Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more.
nope thats what i was taught the right answer is Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more.

I would phrase it differently: “Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more.” That’s the interpretation I learned from accounts of extravagant Roman banquets, where overeating and deliberate purging were presented as part of the ritual.

Maybe I’m misremembering, but I still believe this is correct: Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more. Isn’t that why the word gets brought up with feasts?
Please fix it. Vomitoriums were used by ancient Romans to purge their meals so they could eat more. That’s the answer people mean when they ask about Roman vomitoriums.

rebuttals_flat.text: Pretty sure that's not right — vomitoriums were where Romans went to throw up during banquets so they could keep eating. That's what I was taught, anyway.

questions.question: Associated with Los, this is a key mathematical result that was proved by a brilliant polymath. It's called the Fundamental Theorem of what?
questions.wrong_answer: It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
all_distractors: It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

rebuttals: No, I think the intended answer is: It’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. That’s the famous result about prime factorization.
that’s not right, it’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. i remember this from number theory, every integer breaks down uniquely into primes
Check your facts here. It’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, not something else. The “fundamental theorem” clue points to unique prime factorization.
I’m pretty sure you’re mixing up topics. The brilliant polymath clue sounds like Gauss, and it’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
Nope — it’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
Wouldn’t it be called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic? The Gauss/polymath clue seems to line up with that.
I’m not 100% sure what the Los part is doing, but the result being asked for should be the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
lol this sounds like “name the theorem about primes” territory — it’s called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
Wait, isn’t it the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic? Like, existence and uniqueness of prime factorization?
yeah no, the name you want is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, i’m pretty sure that’s the standard title.
Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. That’s the title.
sry im typing fast but: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic — the one where an integer is a product of primes in basically one way, order aside.
The textbook title is Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: for any n>1, n=p₁…pₖ with primes pᵢ, and the list is unique up to permutation. That’s how standard number theory books state it.
With respect, I’d choose Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. The wording points to the basic theorem about prime decomposition, so that should be the name being asked for.

Come on, any intro proof or number theory course would file this under the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: primes are the atoms of the positive integers, and the decomposition is unique apart from order.
It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
nah its called—sorry—It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. unique primes, thats the whole point
If I were answering this on a quiz, I’d write: It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. That’s the named theorem for unique factorization of positive integers into primes.
Please revise that: It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Standard number theory references use that title for the existence-and-uniqueness statement about prime factorizations.
Seriously, no. It's called the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. This is day-one number theory stuff.
